name: Ibuprofen
meta_description: Learn about ibuprofen: uses, dosage, side effects, warnings, and precautions for this common NSAID pain reliever and anti-inflammatory medication.
description: Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used to reduce fever, pain, and inflammation. It works by blocking the production of prostaglandins, chemicals in the body that cause pain and swelling. It is one of the most widely used over-the-counter medications for mild to moderate pain and is available in various forms including tablets, capsules, liquids, and topical gels.
uses: Relief of mild to moderate pain such as headache, toothache, and backache
Reduction of fever
Treatment of inflammation in conditions like arthritis
Relief of menstrual cramps
Management of muscle aches and joint pain
dosage: Adults and children over 12: 200-400 mg every 4-6 hours as needed, not exceeding 1200 mg per day without medical supervision. Take with food or milk to reduce stomach upset. For children under 12, dosing is based on weight and should be directed by a healthcare provider. Always use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration necessary.
warnings: Do not take if you have a history of stomach ulcers or bleeding
Avoid use in the third trimester of pregnancy
Consult a doctor before use if you have kidney, liver, or heart disease
Do not combine with other NSAIDs or blood thinners without medical advice
Seek immediate medical attention for signs of bleeding, chest pain, or severe allergic reaction
Long-term use may increase the risk of heart attack and stroke

name: Paracetamol
meta_description: Learn about paracetamol (acetaminophen): uses, dosage, side effects, warnings, and safe use of this common pain and fever medication.
description: Paracetamol, also known as acetaminophen, is a widely used analgesic (pain reliever) and antipyretic (fever reducer). Unlike NSAIDs, it does not have significant anti-inflammatory properties. It is generally gentle on the stomach and is the preferred pain reliever for many people who cannot tolerate NSAIDs, including pregnant women and those with stomach ulcers.
uses: Relief of mild to moderate pain including headache and muscle ache
Reduction of fever
Management of cold and flu symptoms
Pain relief for those who cannot take NSAIDs
Suitable for use during pregnancy when recommended by a doctor
dosage: Adults and children over 12: 500-1000 mg every 4-6 hours as needed, not exceeding 4000 mg (4 g) in any 24-hour period. For children, dosing is based on weight and age; always follow the product label or medical advice. Do not exceed the recommended dose, as overdose can cause serious liver damage even if symptoms do not appear immediately.
side_effects: Liver damage in cases of overdose
Low blood platelets with prolonged use (rare)
warnings: Never exceed the maximum daily dose of 4000 mg for adults
Avoid alcohol while taking paracetamol to reduce liver damage risk
Check other medications for paracetamol to avoid accidental overdose
Consult a doctor before use if you have liver disease or drink alcohol regularly
Seek emergency care immediately in case of suspected overdose, even without symptoms
Use with caution in people with low body weight or malnutrition

name: Aspirin
meta_description: Learn about aspirin: uses, dosage, side effects, warnings, and its role in pain relief and cardiovascular protection.
description: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is an NSAID with analgesic, antipyretic, and anti-inflammatory properties. In addition to pain and fever relief, low-dose aspirin is widely used for its blood-thinning effects to prevent heart attacks and strokes in people at high cardiovascular risk. It is one of the oldest and most studied medications in use today.
uses: Relief of mild to moderate pain
Reduction of fever
Anti-inflammatory treatment for conditions like arthritis
Low-dose prevention of heart attacks in high-risk individuals
Prevention of ischemic stroke in certain patients
dosage: For pain or fever in adults: 300-900 mg every 4-6 hours as needed, not exceeding 4000 mg per day. For cardiovascular prevention (only under medical supervision): typically 75-150 mg once daily. Take with food or water to minimize stomach irritation. Never give aspirin to children or teenagers under 16 due to the risk of Reye's syndrome unless specifically directed by a doctor.
side_effects: Increased risk of bleeding and bruising
Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) at high doses
Allergic reactions including asthma exacerbation in sensitive individuals
warnings: Do not give to children or teenagers under 16 due to Reye's syndrome risk
Avoid if you have a bleeding disorder or active stomach ulcer
Consult a doctor before use if pregnant or breastfeeding
Do not combine with other blood thinners without medical advice
Stop taking aspirin and seek care if you experience black stools or vomiting blood
Inform healthcare providers before any surgery or dental procedures

name: Antibiotics
meta_description: Learn about antibiotics: uses, dosage, side effects, warnings, and the importance of responsible use to prevent antibiotic resistance.
description: Antibiotics are a class of medications used to treat bacterial infections by killing or inhibiting the growth of bacteria. They are ineffective against viral infections such as the common cold or flu. There are many types of antibiotics, including penicillins, cephalosporins, macrolides, and fluoroquinolones, each targeting different bacteria and types of infection. Responsible use is essential to prevent antibiotic resistance.
uses: Treatment of bacterial respiratory infections such as strep throat
Management of skin and soft tissue infections
Treatment of urinary tract infections
Treatment of certain sexually transmitted infections
Prevention of infection before surgical procedures
dosage: Dosage varies widely depending on the specific antibiotic, type and severity of infection, and patient factors such as age, weight, and kidney function. Always follow the prescribed course exactly as directed, complete the full course even if you feel better, and never share or save antibiotics for future use. Typical courses range from 5 to 14 days.
side_effects: Yeast infections (oral thrush or vaginal candidiasis)
Allergic reactions ranging from rash to anaphylaxis
Digestive upset and loss of appetite
warnings: Only use antibiotics prescribed for your specific infection
Always complete the full prescribed course even if symptoms improve
Never use leftover antibiotics or share them with others
Inform your doctor of any allergies, especially to penicillin
Misuse contributes to antibiotic resistance, making future infections harder to treat
Seek immediate medical help for signs of severe allergic reaction

name: Antihistamines
meta_description: Learn about antihistamines: uses, dosage, side effects, warnings, and how they relieve allergy symptoms and other conditions.
description: Antihistamines are medications that block the action of histamine, a chemical released by the immune system during allergic reactions. They are commonly used to treat allergies, hay fever, and hives, and some types are also effective for motion sickness, nausea, and as sleep aids. Antihistamines are generally classified as first-generation (which tend to cause drowsiness) and second-generation (which are less sedating).
uses: Relief of seasonal and perennial allergy symptoms
Treatment of hay fever (allergic rhinitis)
Reduction of hives and skin itching
Management of motion sickness and nausea
Use as a short-term sleep aid for some first-generation types
dosage: Dosage depends on the specific antihistamine, formulation, and patient age. Second-generation antihistamines such as cetirizine or loratadine are typically taken once daily (10 mg for adults). First-generation antihistamines may be taken every 4-6 hours but cause drowsiness. Always follow the product label or medical advice, and do not exceed the recommended dose. Adjustments may be needed for children, older adults, or those with kidney or liver conditions.
side_effects: Digestive upset such as nausea or constipation
warnings: Avoid driving or operating machinery if the medication causes drowsiness
Do not combine with alcohol or other sedating medications
Consult a doctor before use if you have glaucoma, enlarged prostate, or thyroid disease
Use with caution in older adults due to increased sensitivity
Inform your doctor of all medications to avoid interactions
Some antihistamines are not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding without medical advice
